rule: For multi-color car models, keep chassis, cabin, hood, wheels, headlights, and windshield as separate leaf nodes instead of merging into a single Compound.
Always include the actual nodes and edges arrays in the JSON response, not just reasoning. Forgetting to output graph data results in an empty viewport.

For airplane models, use a cylinder rotated 90° around Y for the fuselage, cones rotated to match for nose/tail, swept-wing sketches extruded and mirrored for wings, and a triangle sketch rotated 90° around X for the vertical tail fin. Keep all sub-assemblies as separate leaf nodes for multi-color rendering.

For animal models like cats, use chamfered boxes for bodies, spheres for heads, extruded triangle sketches for ears, and lofted tapers for tails. Keep all parts as separate leaf nodes for multi-color rendering.
Simple flat sketches extruded for car bodies look bad; use chamfered boxes for sleeker automotive forms.

When building multi-part objects like flowers, keep each sub-assembly as a separate leaf node (no outgoing Compound/Boolean) to enable multi-color rendering. Use CircularPattern with a single source shape for radial petal arrays.

A simple cylinder + lofted cone + extruded triangle fin rocket is visually unappealing. When building rockets, use more sophisticated shaping: chamfered body stages, detailed engine nozzles via lofted profiles, swept-back fins via curved sketches, and add surface details like panel lines or rivet patterns using SubdivideSurface.
For realistic animal models, use lofted tapered profiles for legs and tails instead of uniform cylinders, and use sphere primitives for organic body parts (chest, head, snout) instead of boxes.
Always include the actual nodes and edges arrays in the JSON response, not just reasoning. Forgetting to output graph data results in an empty viewport.

When a user rejects a rocket design as too simple, immediately pivot to a more sophisticated style using tapered lofted bodies, clustered lofted engine nozzles, swept sketch fins, and panel-line surface detailing; keep sub-assemblies as separate leaf nodes for multi-color rendering.

ScaleXYZ and non-uniform Ellipsoid (radii not all equal) are fully reliable as of July 2026 — they now produce a real, correctly-sized/volumed shape (fixed a silent no-op bug). Prefer Ellipsoid, or Sphere->ScaleXYZ, over flat extruded sketches for petals, leaves, seeds, eggs, pebbles, cushions, gems. The result is faceted (polyhedral) rather than perfectly smooth, invisible at normal render size, but avoid stacking a tight Fillet directly on a coarse/small ScaleXYZ result. Budget roughly 0.1-1.5s of extra evaluation per use — a full flower with 16 non-uniform petals still builds in under 2s, so use them freely.
comments: Added after root-causing why petals looked like unscaled spheres: BRepBuilderAPI_GTransform is not compiled into this WASM build. Fix rebuilds shapes from a deformed triangle mesh instead.

rule: New (July 9 2026): Bend (axis X/Y/Z, angle) curves a shape like a banana around its own center — use for petals/leaves/wings curving upward (axis X or Y) or stems/horns curving sideways as they rise (axis Z). Twist (axis, angle) spirals a shape along its own extent — drill bits, spiral columns, horns, flames, twisted petals. Both budget roughly 1-8s depending on angle and how many original faces the input has (a smooth Ellipsoid-derived shape is fast and near-exact; a coarse plain Box/Cylinder is slower and loses a few percent volume to faceting — bend/twist organic shapes, not primitive boxes, when precision matters). Pipe (pathSvg, radius) makes a circular tube along an SVG-style path (same M/L/C/Q syntax as Sketch, omit the closing Z) — use for stems, vines, cables, tentacles, horns instead of chaining Sketch+Extrude+Rotate hacks.
